subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

reviews.author: Doug Anderson
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson expressed concern that the SDEI NMI handler may not be able to handle CPUs that are in a wedged state, as it relies on firmware delivering the event regardless of the target's DAIF (Device Access Interrupt Flag), but this assumption may not hold for all firmware implementations.

Gave Reviewed-by
sentiment_signals: concern about SDEI NMI handler reliability
reviews.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 17, 2026 at 12:20 PM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

2026-06-17.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPU wedging by escalating smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. When a CPU is wedged with interrupts masked, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 at the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF (Debug Access Interface Flag). This allows the CPU to reach a wedged CPU and acknowledge by going offline. The stop bookkeeping is also folded into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() for consistency.

2026-06-18.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPU wedging by escalating smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. When a CPU is wedged with interrupts masked, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 at the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF (Debug Access Interface Flag). This allows the CPU to reach a wedged CPU and acknowledge by going offline. The stop bookkeeping is also folded into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() for consistency.
